papers.title: Risk assessment of flange climb derailment of a rail vehicle - ADS
papers.abstract: We study the wheel flange climb onto the railhead, which is one of the most dangerous regimes of motion and can lead to derailment. The tangential components of the wheel-rail interaction forces are described by the creep model with small slips taken into account. We pass to the limit of infinite rigidity of the interacting bodies (zero slip velocities). It is shown that, in the actual service conditions of rail vehicle motion, neglecting the wheel-rail slip is not justified; namely, the limit model is determined by the primary Dirac constraints, i.e., finite relations between coordinates and

papers.abstract: Logistic regression is a statistical method used for solving classification tasks. It models the probability of an outcome, which is usually binary. However, it can also be applied when the outcome variable has more than two categories, allowing for the modelling of multiple classes by estimating the probability of each category given the values of predictor (explanatory) variables . Our outcome variable, accident rate, has three categories; therefore, logistic regression estimates the probability of each level of accident rate in a certain month, given the values of all explanatory variables

papers.abstract:
In plan view, track consists of straight, curved and transitional sections designed to ensure safety against derailment and maintaining comfort for passengers. For low speed train operation, transitions are not necessary. However, as speed increases, transitions between straight and curves are required. A common method for setting out track alignment is by versine as this can measured using simple chord and offset process, (figure 2-1).

papers.title: A 3D Contact Force Safety Criterion for Flange Climb Derailment of a Railway Wheel | Semantic Scholar
papers.abstract:
A revision on the railway safety criteria evolution has shown that the fundamentals of wheel/rail contact phenomena have not been completely addressed. Limitations encountered on the available criteria for safety against derailment have motivated a deeper analysis on the wheel rolling mechanism. The contact mechanics were completely modelled with two tangential components, lateral and longitudinal forces, including spinning effects. An extended three-dimensional formula for the railway wheel…

Derailment leads to a loss of vehicle guidance, and hence, the avoidance of derailment is vital to the safe operation of railways. Reasons behind derailment include component failure or high lateral forces developed at large angles of attack between the wheel and rail, which are typically present in smaller-radius curves. Such forces can lead to the wheel flange climbing out of the rail, gauge widening, rail rollover and track panel shift. In addition to t

Derailments happen. The minor ones cause damage and disruption; the big ones make the news.  While there are derailments attributed to a single vehicle or track cause, often, the cause is the result of a combination of vehicle- and track-related contributing factors, underscoring that vehicle/track interaction is a system.

research_gaps: Limited ML/AI application to derailment probability prediction
Insufficient digital twin models for real-time track monitoring
Lack of climate-change impact studies on track geometry
Limited sensor-fusion approaches for early derailment warning
Sparse research on derailment risk in mixed-speed traffic corridors
Emerging need for autonomous inspection system validation frameworks
key_findings: Derailment safety in railway engineering focuses on wheel-rail contact mechanics, with peer-reviewed research emphasizing the importance of wheel/rail angle and lateral forces to prevent derailment
Flange climb derailment is a significant concern, where high lateral forces cause wheel climbing
Safety measures include profile management and maintenance guidelines
Track geometry irregularities significantly impact railway safety and can lead to derailments
Peer-reviewed research emphasizes the importance of assessing track geometry to prevent accidents
Regular monitoring and maintenance are crucial for safe rail operations
Derailment probability modeling uses statistical analysis to estimate risks based on train length, speed, and operational practices
Peer-reviewed research quantifies benefits of safety improvements
Logistic regression and temporal analysis are common methods
High-speed rail dynamics simulation focuses on derailment safety, with peer-reviewed research emphasizing risk diagnosis models and aerodynamic assessments
